title: Weyl-type hybrid subconvexity bounds for twisted L-functions and Heegner points on shrinking sets
speaker: Matthew Young
affiliation: Texas A & M University; von Neumann Fellow, School of Mathematics
abstract: One of the major themes of the analytic theory of automorphic forms is the connection between equidistribution and subconvexity. An early example of this is the famous result of Duke showing the equidistribution of Heegner points on the modular surface, a problem that boils down to the subconvexity problem for the quadratic twists of Hecke-Maass L-functions. It is interesting to understand if the Heegner points also equidistribute on finer scales, a question that leads one to seek strong bounds on a large collection of central values.

proof_stages.label: Weyl-type subconvexity benchmark
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Burgess's character-sum and Dirichlet-L-function bounds and Conrey-Iwaniec's quadratic-twist improvements, setting the Weyl-type exponent as the target and raising the issue of dependence on spectral or weight parameters.

proof_stages.label: Hybrid cubic-moment theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem bounds short Maass spectral and holomorphic weight cubic moments of central quadratic twists uniformly by qT^{1+epsilon} or qk^{1+epsilon}, with nonnegativity allowing individual Weyl-type subconvex bounds and comparison to earlier fixed-aspect results.

proof_stages.label: Shrinking Heegner-point equidistribution statement
proof_stages.summary: Heegner points are recalled via binary quadratic forms, Duke-Iwaniec equidistribution is reviewed, and a uniform theorem is stated for varying smooth test functions whose Laplacian derivatives are controlled by a shrinking parameter V.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic and geometric consequences of shrinking uniformity
proof_stages.summary: The shrinking-set theorem is converted into concrete corollaries about congruence solutions, effective upper bounds for points in small neighborhoods, and Heegner points reaching cusp regions at controlled rates.

proof_stages.label: Spectral expansion and Waldspurger reduction
proof_stages.summary: Expanding the test function spectrally isolates the constant eigenfunction as the expected volume main term, while the Zhang/Waldspurger formula expresses the squared nonconstant Heegner periods in terms of central L-values.

proof_stages.label: Spectral truncation and cubic-moment error bound
proof_stages.summary: Repeated integration by parts with the Laplacian truncates the spectral expansion at size about Vd^epsilon, and Hölder's inequality together with the cubic-moment theorem bounds the resulting error term.

proof_stages.label: Approximate functional equation and Kuznetsov setup
proof_stages.summary: For the cubic moment, approximate functional equations produce triple sums of Hecke eigenvalues twisted by quadratic characters, and Kuznetsov/Petersson summation converts the spectral average into Kloosterman sums with a Bessel-transform weight treated uniformly in the Maass and holomorphic cases.

proof_stages.label: Poisson summation, phase cancellation, and variable separation
proof_stages.summary: After localizing variables and applying threefold Poisson summation, the arithmetic complete sum and the Archimedean oscillatory integral are analyzed in parallel, with an additive phase from the character sum canceled by stationary phase and the remaining variable block separated by multiplicative characters/Fourier inversion.

examples.name: Quadratic residues and nonresidues in short intervals
examples.note: Burgess's character-sum cancellation is illustrated by guaranteeing quadratic residues, nonresidues, and primitive roots in sufficiently short intervals; this motivates the meaning of cancellation but is not used in the later proof.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies Burgess’s character-sum bound to a quadratic character mod a prime and explains how cancellation in a short interval forces both residues and nonresidues to occur

passes.reason: Ordinary quadratic residue classes in intervals are used as a Burgess-bound application, not as a sporadic or adversarial object.

examples.name: Smoothed square of side 1/V
examples.note: A shrinking square, smoothed to a cutoff function, illustrates why applying the Laplacian n times should cost a factor V^{2n}.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs a smoothed characteristic function for a square of side 1/V and checks how derivatives and Laplacian powers grow like V^{2n}

examples.name: Congruence solution with a or b of size D^eta
examples.note: The corollary translates a shrinking cusp-window statement for Heegner points into elementary-looking congruences b^2 congruent to -D modulo 4a with controlled variable size.
examples.engagement_reason: he derives how the congruence b^2≡-D mod 4a corresponds to a binary quadratic form and later relates a of size D^η to Heegner-point height via Im τ=√D/(2a)

passes.reason: Forcing a coefficient to have size D^eta probes an edge/cusp range of the Heegner-point parametrization.

examples.name: Fourier shrink-spread duality
examples.note: The analogy with a Fourier transform spreading when the original function is squeezed explains why shrinking the geometric set forces more spectral terms to appear.
examples.engagement_reason: he demonstrates the shrink-spread principle by bounding spectral coefficients through repeated integration by parts and showing shrinking at scale 1/V forces spectral support out to about V
